notes: SVD derivation connects eigenvalues (earlier in course) to factorization to geometry; whiteboard-heavy

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 6 - CNN, RNN, and Attention
notes: CNN -> RNN -> attention built incrementally in same lecture; attention derived from prior concepts

notes: EBM framing introduced early and reused throughout; self-supervised builds on EBM concepts

title: 1. Algorithmic Thinking, Peak Finding
notes: 1D → 2D peak-finding reused in sorting and graph lectures later in course

title: 3. Insertion Sort, Merge Sort
notes: Merge operation and recurrence are prerequisites for FFT and divide-and-conquer later

title: 4. Heaps and Heap Sort
notes: Heap structure reused in Dijkstra and priority-queue-based shortest path algorithms

title: 9. Table Doubling, Karp-Rabin
notes: Rolling-hash resurfaces in open addressing; amortized analysis connects to earlier hashing

notes: Topological order is foundation for DP-on-DAG; cross-references BFS from 6.006 SP20

title: 4. Hashing
instructor: Erik Demaine, Jason Ku, Justin Solomon
notes: Hashing underpins linear sorting; connects to set interface from earlier lectures

title: 7. Binary Trees, Part 2 (AVL)
instructor: Erik Demaine, Jason Ku, Justin Solomon
notes: AVL balance argument reused in skip lists; augmentation pattern reused throughout

title: 9. Breadth-First Search
instructor: Erik Demaine, Jason Ku, Justin Solomon
notes: BFS entry point to all graph-search lectures; proof structure reused verbatim for Dijkstra

title: 11. Weighted Shortest Paths
instructor: Erik Demaine, Jason Ku, Justin Solomon
notes: Relaxation framework is common thread through Bellman-Ford, Dijkstra, Johnson's

title: 15. Dynamic Programming, Part 1
instructor: Erik Demaine, Jason Ku, Justin Solomon
notes: SRTBOT is skeleton for all DP lectures; subproblem DAG connects back to topological sort

title: 3. Divide & Conquer — FFT
course: MIT 6.046J Design and Analysis of Algorithms, Spring 2015
notes: FFT builds on D&C from Lec 2; reappears in number-theoretic algorithms later in course

course: MIT 6.046J Design and Analysis of Algorithms, Spring 2015
notes: Indicator RVs and linearity of expectation used across randomized algorithm lectures

title: 12. Greedy Algorithms — MST
course: MIT 6.046J Design and Analysis of Algorithms, Spring 2015
notes: Cut/cycle property; union-find resurfaces in incremental graph algorithms

course: MIT 6.046J Design and Analysis of Algorithms, Spring 2015
notes: Foundation for bipartite matching; max-flow min-cut connects to LP duality

title: 16. Complexity — P, NP, NP-completeness
course: MIT 6.046J Design and Analysis of Algorithms, Spring 2015
notes: Reductions connect algorithms from whole course to decision-problem analogs

title: 1. Geometry of Linear Equations
notes: Row/column duality is geometric foundation for nullspace, four subspaces, projections

title: 4. Factorization into A = LU
notes: LU is base factorization referenced in determinants, eigenvalues, and SVD lectures

title: 6. Column Space and Nullspace
notes: Column and null spaces are two of the four fundamental subspaces; prerequisite for projections

title: 10. The Four Fundamental Subspaces
notes: Unifies all subspaces from Lec 6–9; pivot lecture for projections, least squares, and SVD

title: 15. Projections onto Subspaces
notes: Projection matrix synthesizes column space and orthogonality; leads to least squares and Gram-Schmidt

title: 21. Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
notes: Characteristic polynomial; feeds into symmetric matrices, differential equations, and SVD

title: 25. Symmetric Matrices and Positive Definiteness
notes: Spectral theorem; PD tests connect eigenvalues to optimization minima and SVD

title: 22. Gradient Descent
course: MIT 18.065 Matrix Methods in Data Analysis, Signal Processing, and Machine Learning, Spring 2018
notes: Convergence rate as function of condition number κ = λ_max/λ_min; previews SGD in NYU DL Week 2

title: 26. Structure of Neural Nets for Deep Learning
course: MIT 18.065 Matrix Methods in Data Analysis, Signal Processing, and Machine Learning, Spring 2018
notes: SVD-based analysis of neural net weights; connects to NYU DL Week 2 backprop

title: 4. Search: DFS, Hill Climbing, Beam
notes: Search algorithms extended with heuristics in Lec 5 (A*); recycled as substrate for game playing in Lec 6

title: 5. Search: Optimal, Branch and Bound, A*
notes: Builds on Lec 4; A* heuristic design connects to constraint propagation in Lec 8

title: 6. Search: Games, Minimax, Alpha-Beta
notes: Minimax is DFS from Lec 4; alpha-beta pruning is branch-and-bound from Lec 5

title: 17. Learning: Boosting
notes: AdaBoost meta-algorithm over weak classifiers; exponential loss connects to probabilistic inference

title: 2. Induction
course: MIT 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science, Fall 2010
notes: Weak and strong induction; examples reappear in graphs and probability lectures

title: 4. Number Theory I
course: MIT 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science, Fall 2010
notes: GCD, Bézout, modular arithmetic; foundation for RSA and 6.046J cryptography lectures

title: 6. Graph Theory and Coloring
course: MIT 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science, Fall 2010
notes: Graph coloring connects to matching, MST, and scheduling in algorithms courses

title: 18. Probability Introduction
course: MIT 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science, Fall 2010
notes: Probability axioms are foundation for conditional probability, independence, and RV lectures

title: 22. Expectation I
course: MIT 6.042J Mathematics for Computer Science, Fall 2010
notes: Linearity of expectation is the analytical engine behind probabilistic algorithm analysis

title: 3. Parametric Inference
notes: Exponential families reappear in MLE and GLMs; sufficient statistics connect to Bayesian sufficiency

title: 7. Hypothesis Testing
notes: Test framework applied to goodness-of-fit, regression, and Bayesian alternatives later

title: 13. Regression
notes: OLS is statistical interpretation of projection from 18.06 Lec 15; inference uses Lec 7 framework

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 1 — History and Motivation
notes: Backprop motivation and hierarchical features built upon in every subsequent week

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 2 — SGD and Backpropagation
notes: Backprop connects Week 1 motivation to Weeks 3–5 architectures; SGD analysis in Week 5

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 3 — CNNs and Parameter Transformations
notes: CNN parameter-sharing is foundation for Week 6 CNN→RNN→Attention progression

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 5 — Optimization for Deep Learning
notes: Directly extends SGD from Week 2; optimization methods used in training all subsequent architectures

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 9 — World Models and Recurrent Nets
notes: DrSAE connects autoencoders (Week 7) with RNNs (Week 6); predictive coding precedes EBM

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 10 — Self-Supervised Learning in CV
notes: SSL extends EBMs (Week 7); transfer reuses feature representations discussed since Week 3

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 11 — Activation and Loss Functions
notes: Loss functions used across course; connection to optimization (Week 5) made explicit

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 12 — NLP and Transformers
notes: Transformers generalize attention from Week 6; LM pretraining connects to SSL (Week 10)

title: NYU Deep Learning Week 13 — Graph Convolutional Networks
notes: GCNs generalize CNNs (Week 3) to non-Euclidean data; spectral view connects to Fourier convolution

title: Lecture 3: Locally Weighted Regression, Logistic Regression
course: Stanford CS229 Machine Learning, Fall 2008 (Stanford Engineering Everywhere)
notes: Logistic regression as GLM in Lec 4; overfitting precursor to learning theory (Lec 9–11)

title: Lecture 7: Optimal Margin Classifier, Kernels
course: Stanford CS229 Machine Learning, Fall 2008 (Stanford Engineering Everywhere)
notes: Hard-margin SVM with KKT conditions; kernel trick made rigorous in Lec 8; Lagrange duality

title: Lecture 12: EM Algorithm
course: Stanford CS229 Machine Learning, Fall 2008 (Stanford Engineering Everywhere)
notes: Jensen's inequality introduced, then used to prove EM convergence; GMM builds on Gaussian analysis

title: Lecture 14: Factor Analysis, PCA, Dimensionality Reduction
course: Stanford CS229 Machine Learning, Fall 2008 (Stanford Engineering Everywhere)
notes: PCA as truncated SVD bridges to SVD content in MIT 18.065 Lec 6 and 18.06 Lec 25

title: 5. CF Pumping Lemma, Turing Machines
notes: TM model introduced, then halting problem proof reuses TM definition — tight within-lecture multi-hop

title: L14: Cache Implementation
notes: Locality principle introduced early; cache hierarchy and replacement policies justified by revisiting it

title: Session 7: Memory Hierarchy Models
notes: Cache-oblivious algorithms; B-trees and van Emde Boas trees analyzed via memory hierarchy model

title: 8. Web Security Model
notes: Cookies and same-origin policy introduced first half; XSS and CSRF in second half exploit the same mechanisms

title: 16. Markov Chains — Steady State and Absorption
course: MIT 6.041 Probabilistic Systems Analysis and Applied Probability, Fall 2010
notes: Transition matrix introduced; steady-state uses eigenvalue theory and Chapman-Kolmogorov derived earlier in same lecture
